behaviors.description: The embedding vector produced for a given query string has the correct dimensionality for the vector database index.
behaviors.justification: A mock embedding model would pass even if the real embedding service changes its output dimension (e.g., model upgrade from 384 to 1536 dims). The test must call a real embedding API (or test instance) to verify actual dimensionality, catching production model drift that a Unit mock would hide.
behaviors.test_description: Call the real embedding API with multiple test queries. Verify each returned vector has the expected dimensionality matching the vector DB schema (e.g., 1536 for OpenAI or 384 for sentence-transformers). Test that dimension validation fails gracefully if embedding service returns unexpected dimensions.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Integration is necessary: a mocked embedding model returning a hardcoded correct-sized vector would miss failures when the real API changes dimension.

behaviors.description: The similarity search returns results ordered by descending cosine similarity score.
behaviors.justification: A mock vector database could return results in any order, hiding real failures where the vector DB doesn't return ordered results or the code doesn't maintain order. Real database interaction with actual similarity calculations is required to catch ordering bugs and DB behavior mismatches.
behaviors.test_description: Insert test vectors into a real vector database with known similarity relationships (e.g., 5 product vectors with calculated pairwise similarities). Query with a test vector and verify results are returned in descending similarity score order. Test boundary cases: single result, ties in similarity scores, and empty result set.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Integration is required: mocking DB ordering would test sorting code, not actual database ranking behavior or whether similarity calculations are correct.

behaviors.description: The LLM re-ranking step changes the order of at least some results compared to the raw similarity ranking when the query has nuanced intent.
behaviors.justification: Correctness depends on real LLM reasoning about nuanced intent. Mocking the LLM to return any order would always pass, hiding whether the real LLM understands the query's nuance or whether the re-ranking prompt is flawed. Different LLMs will rank differently; real model output is required.
behaviors.test_description: End-to-end test with a query demonstrating nuanced intent (e.g., 'lightweight fast laptop' with inherent trade-offs). Capture initial similarity ranking from vector search. Call the real LLM re-ranking API with the original query and search results. Verify re-ranked order differs and reflects nuanced-intent alignment (e.g., results balancing both lightweight and fast moved higher).
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Agentic is necessary: only a real LLM can demonstrate understanding of nuanced intent; a mock could trivially shuffle order without proving intent-awareness, hiding flawed re-ranking prompts.

behaviors.description: A query with no close matches in the database returns an empty result set rather than low-relevance noise.
behaviors.justification: The behavior depends on a similarity threshold (in vector search or post-search filtering). A mock database could hide threshold logic. Real database interaction with actual similarity scores is necessary to verify that low-relevance results are filtered out, not returned as noise.
behaviors.test_description: Query the real vector database with terms known to have no close semantic matches (e.g., a random nonsense term or domain mismatch). Verify the result set is empty or that all returned results exceed a documented similarity threshold. Test boundary cases: a result at exactly the threshold, and a result just below it (should be excluded).
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Integration is sufficient: the threshold filtering is tested against real similarity calculations. If LLM re-ranking also filters low-confidence results, that would escalate to Agentic, but the base filtering behavior is Integration.

behaviors.description: The end-to-end latency from receiving the query to returning re-ranked results stays below 2 seconds for the median case.
behaviors.justification: Latency depends on multiple real, sequential agentic steps: embedding inference, vector DB query, and LLM re-ranking. Mocking any step would hide real bottlenecks (e.g., slow LLM API). Real end-to-end execution with representative workload is required to measure true performance.
behaviors.test_description: Execute end-to-end queries (embedding → similarity search → LLM re-ranking) with representative product data and query distribution. Measure round-trip latency for at least 100 diverse queries. Verify median latency is below 2 seconds. Include outlier analysis: identify slowest components (embedding service vs. LLM vs. vector DB) to guide optimization.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Workflow is required: all three agentic components must run in sequence with real timing. Testing embedding + mock LLM would miss LLM latency; testing search + mock embedding would miss embedding latency. Real end-to-end is the only way to catch the SLA violation.
